claim: Post-synthesis, the best reading is that this market should resolve YES because the stronger and better-audited source-specific evidence points to the contract-named NASA March 2026 cell being 128 = 1.28°C, inside the bracket; however, independent verification quality is only medium because the bundle contains direct raw-lane disagreement about that exact cell and my synthesis-stage verification was bounded by network access limits, so I compress slightly below the most bullish lanes rather than endorse the full 99% view.

frontmatter.next_checkpoint: formal market settlement / any resolver dispute note on the NASA March 2026 row

sections.Alpha summary: Market-implied probability is 0.949. My syndicated range is 0.90 to 0.97 YES. That is broadly consistent with market, with at most a marginal edge and not a strong actionable divergence. The likely reason the market is near-correct is that this contract is mostly mechanical once the contract-named NASA value is live; the only thing that keeps me below the strongest YES lanes is unresolved audit conflict across personas over the exact row reading.
sections.Input coverage: All five personas were available: base-rate, catalyst-hunter, market-implied, risk-manager, and variant-view. None were missing. I used the raw persona findings as canonical and compared them against the sidecars. Supporting assumption/evidence artifacts were not necessary beyond what was already preserved in the raw findings because the core issue was a direct contradiction over the same named NASA cell. Coverage is complete because all planned personas ran and the key disagreement is explicit rather than hidden by missing lanes.
sections.Market-implied baseline: The market-implied baseline is 0.949 YES as of 2026-04-09T18:32:57Z. The market was already pricing this as effectively near-settled, which is a meaningful prior in a source-of-truth contract but not decisive given the lane-level conflict over the exact NASA row.
sections.Syndicated probability estimate: My final post-synthesis estimate is 0.90 to 0.97 YES. The center of gravity remains YES because the two strongest pro-market lanes explicitly report direct row captures of `2026 108 124 128 ...`, and even the catalyst-hunter lane, while unable to secure a perfect in-run row capture, ended up directionally No only because it inferred from proposed-outcome state rather than from a cleaner direct table audit. The bearish lanes are therefore not a coherent climate countercase; they are an audit-conflict countercase centered on whether the NASA row was actually 130/134 instead of 128.
sections.Difference from swarm-implied center: This differs materially from the provisional swarm center implied by the lane outputs because that swarm center was distorted by a hard split between 0.03/0.08 NO-style lanes and 0.99 YES-style lanes. After critical review, I do not think the bearish lanes earned equal weight. Their disagreement is driven by mutually inconsistent direct-source readings of the same exact NASA cell, while the bullish lanes provide more specific extraction detail (`2026 108 124 128 ...`) and better explain the mapping from table units to settlement. I still moved somewhat below the most bullish 0.99 views because I could not independently reproduce the row fetch during synthesis due to network limits, so the edge was not verified strongly enough to fully endorse the extreme YES tail.
sections.Agreement or disagreement with market: I broadly agree with the market. My range crosses slightly below the market but is centered near it, reflecting that the market is probably pricing the right mechanism: the named NASA row appears to be inside the bracket, and the residual uncertainty is mostly dispute/process risk. I do not see a robust independently verified reason to be materially below market once the raw findings are weighed for audit quality.
sections.Independent verification of edge: Independent verification quality is medium. I independently checked the raw persona findings rather than trusting sidecars, and that review materially changed the interpretation of the swarm because it revealed that the disagreement was not about climate mechanics but about contradictory source extraction. I also attempted synthesis-stage direct retrieval of the NASA file, but host network access failed, so I could not fully arbitrate the row myself from first principles during this run. What was independently checked: the raw lane provenance, the exact claims each lane made about the NASA row, and whether sidecars faithfully represented those claims. What remains weak: a synthesis-stage direct archive-grade pull of the NASA table and publication-timestamp evidence. That is why verification quality is medium rather than high.
sections.Compression toward market due to verific: Yes. I compressed toward market because the apparent edge from the bearish swarm lanes could not be independently verified strongly enough. The provisional swarm outputs implied a massive below-market view, but that huge edge depended on trusting lane claims that the same NASA cell read 130 or 134. I could not independently reproduce those values, and the better-specified opposing lanes said 128 with explicit row text. Because the bearish edge failed the very-high verification bar required for such a large market gap, I reverted materially toward the market and away from the swarm median.
sections.Timing and catalyst posture: The key checkpoint is formal settlement or any resolver/dispute note clarifying the governing NASA March 2026 row. The edge is more likely to compress than widen before then, because once a source-specific market is near resolution, remaining uncertainty tends to collapse into audit/process handling. Waiting likely improves confidence but probably does not create much new alpha unless a dispute emerges.
sections.Decision blockers: The main blocker to high-confidence conviction is unresolved direct-source audit conflict: different personas reported 128, 130, and 134 for the same named NASA row/column. A secondary blocker is that synthesis-stage external verification was constrained by failed network access, so I could not settle the row conflict independently. There is also minor contract ambiguity from the February fallback typo, but that looks less important than the row-reading conflict itself.
sections.Implication for the question: Operationally, the question is best read as a contract lookup rather than a climate forecast. If the correct named NASA March 2026 cell is 128, the market resolves YES; if the bearish lanes' alternate row reads were right, it resolves NO. Post-synthesis, the best-supported interpretation is that the operative value is 1.28°C and therefore inside the bracket.
sections.Consensus across personas: Multiple personas converged on several points: the contract is governed by one exact NASA GISTEMP text-table cell; later revisions do not matter once the first value is available; broader climate narrative is secondary to source-of-truth mechanics; the fallback clause contains a February/March drafting issue that creates some process risk; and whatever edge exists now is mostly about settlement mechanics, not fresh climate uncertainty.
sections.Key disagreements across personas: The dominant disagreement is factual and source-of-truth based: market-implied and variant-view say the NASA row is 128; risk-manager says 130; base-rate says 134. A second disagreement is weighting-based: whether the market's 94.9% price should be treated as corroborating evidence in a near-settled source-specific contract or as a sign of collective misread. A third disagreement is timing/process-based but smaller: how much tail risk to assign to the malformed February fallback clause and publication timing.
sections.Best countercase: The best countercase is the risk-manager/base-rate thesis: this market may be badly mispriced because traders are directionally right that March was warm but wrong on the exact contract cell, which may have landed just above the bracket. That countercase is real because it points to a source-audit failure mode rather than a weak narrative objection. Risk-manager represented the sharpest version of it; base-rate was more confident but also the most outlying on the exact number.
sections.Encapsulated assumptions: Shared assumptions: the contract uses the NASA GISS `GLB.Ts+dSST.txt` row 2026 column Mar; later revisions do not matter; fallback only matters if the primary source is unavailable. Contested assumptions: whether the visible operative March 2026 row is 128, 130, or 134; whether the market/proposed-outcome state reflects a correct row reading; whether publication timing could matter in a dispute. Fragile assumptions: that no hidden resolver clarification exists and that the February fallback typo remains non-operative.
sections.Encapsulated evidence map: Strongest supporting evidence for YES: market-implied and variant-view both report the exact row fragment `2026 108 124 128 ...`; both tie that directly to the contract-named source and unit conversion. Strongest contradictory evidence: base-rate and risk-manager claim direct reads of 134 and 130 respectively, which would both imply NO. Authoritative source-of-truth evidence: the contract text naming the NASA table and exact row/column, plus repeated agreement that this is the operative settlement surface. Ambiguous evidence: market price and proposed-outcome state, which can corroborate but cannot by themselves settle an underlying row-reading conflict.
sections.Evidence weighting: I gave the most weight to direct-source claims that were both specific and mechanically explained. That favored market-implied and variant-view over the bearish lanes. I downweighted generic climate-context evidence from NOAA/Berkeley because it does not settle a narrow bracket lookup. I also downweighted pure market-state inference because a proposed outcome or price can reflect trader consensus without proving the row extraction. I did not ignore the bearish lanes; I used them primarily as a disconfirming audit challenge and as the reason to keep the range below 0.99 certainty.
sections.Counterpoints / strongest disconfirming: The strongest disconfirming evidence is that multiple serious lanes claimed conflicting direct reads of the same NASA table cell. That is much stronger than a token caveat because if the bearish read were right, the contract outcome flips immediately. The inability to perform a clean synthesis-stage external row capture keeps that disconfirming case alive.
sections.Resolution or source-of-truth interpreta: The source-of-truth interpretation is straightforward in principle: use NASA GISS `GLB.Ts+dSST.txt`, row 2026, column Mar, in 0.01°C units, and ignore later revisions. The unresolved issue is not contract interpretation so much as source extraction reliability across lanes. The February fallback reference looks like drafting noise and should matter only if the primary March source were unavailable or a dispute elevated the typo, which none of the lanes established as likely.
sections.Why this could create or destroy alpha: This could create alpha only if the market materially misread the named NASA cell or if traders underpriced dispute risk. But after synthesis, the huge anti-market edge suggested by the swarm median looks untrustworthy because it was not independently verified and seems driven by contradictory row reads. That destroys most of the apparent alpha. The remaining edge, if any, is small and mostly about settlement friction rather than event uncertainty.
sections.What would falsify this interpretation /: A clean archive-grade capture of the NASA table showing the operative March 2026 cell as 130 or 134 would sharply move the view below market. Likewise, an exchange clarification that the visible 128 row was not the governing first-available posting would weaken the YES case materially. Conversely, a resolver note or archived NASA pull confirming `2026 Mar = 128` would push the estimate up toward the 0.98-0.99 range.
sections.Highest-value next research: The single highest-value next check is one archive-grade retrieval or screenshot of the exact NASA `GLB.Ts+dSST.txt` 2026 row with timestamp/provenance sufficient to settle whether March is 128, 130, or 134.
sections.Source-quality assessment: The primary source class is excellent in theory: the exact NASA table named by the contract. The most important secondary source class is contract text / market-state context, which explains what counts but does not substitute for the number itself. Evidence independence is medium at best because the decisive evidence is concentrated in one source-of-truth surface. Source-of-truth ambiguity is medium, not because the rules are mostly unclear, but because upstream sourcing across lanes shows thin reproducibility on the critical row extraction. The synthesis is somewhat bottlenecked by that lane-level audit inconsistency.
sections.Verification impact: Yes, additional synthesis-layer verification was used in the form of raw-finding review and sidecar-vs-raw consistency checking, and it materially changed confidence. Cross-lane comparison exposed that the provisional swarm center was not a reliable summary because the disagreement was driven by contradictory readings of the same source. The synthesis also exposed a provenance weakness: some lanes gave exact row text while others reported conflicting values without enough extra arbitration to settle them. No successful direct external NASA refetch was completed during synthesis because of network-access failure.
sections.Persona contribution map: base-rate — strongest pure anti-market case; emphasized mechanical contract reading and claimed 1.34°C, but now looks potentially overconfident relative to unresolved source conflict. catalyst-hunter — useful on timing/catalyst collapse and on the idea that this is mostly a post-publication audit problem; weaker on exact row verification. market-implied — strongest pro-market case with the clearest specific row extract and solid explanation of why market price could be efficient here. risk-manager — preserved the best countercase that the exact cell might miss the bracket high; valuable mainly as an audit-warning lane. variant-view — strongest concise YES variant focused on the named row = 128 and residual mechanics tail only.
sections.Reusable lesson signals: Possible durable lesson: in source-specific resolution markets, swarm synthesis should heavily privilege reproducible row-level captures over summary confidence. Possible underbuilt driver: extraction/audit reproducibility may deserve more explicit treatment in future narrow-bracket markets. Possible source-quality lesson: a huge swarm-vs-market edge built on contradictory readings of the same table should default to skepticism and compression. Confidence this lesson is reusable: high.
sections.Orchestrator review suggestions: review later for durable lesson: yes; review later for driver candidate: yes; review later for canon or linkage issue: no; review later for swarm-method issue: yes. Reason: this case is a strong example where lane disagreement on a single authoritative cell should have triggered stronger artifact-grade source capture before allowing an extreme provisional swarm edge to anchor synthesis.
sections.Recommended follow-up: Collect missing source provenance via one clean NASA row capture or archive reference, then request decision-maker review only if that check still leaves nontrivial dispute risk. If no dispute note appears and the 128 row is confirmed, no further climate research is needed.
